facts.fact: Binary search on answer space works when the feasibility condition is monotonic: if x works, then all greater values also work (or vice versa)
Default load factor for HashMap is 0.75, representing a balance between memory density and collision frequency
Java 8+ HashMap uses red-black trees (not linked lists) for collision chains when bucket contains 8+ entries, improving worst-case search from O(n) to O(log n)
AVL trees have worst-case height bounded by approximately 1.44 log n, while red-black trees bound height at 2 log n
Red-black trees require at most 2 rotations after insertion and 3 rotations after deletion; AVL trees require many more rotations
Tries search time is O(M) where M is key length, independent of number of keys; BST requires O(M log N)
Doubling array capacity ensures appending n elements takes O(n) total time, making each append O(1) amortized
When resizing HashMap, if current capacity is 16, rehashing occurs when 12 buckets are full (0.75 × 16)
Separate chaining can operate at load factor up to 100%, while open addressing typically maintains 50% load factor for performance
Cache performance favors open addressing over chaining because contiguous memory access in open addressing enables CPU cache hits
Fibonacci DP can be reduced to O(1) space by maintaining only two variables: prev and cur, updating them via simultaneous assignment
0/1 Knapsack space can be optimized from O(n×W) to O(W) using a single 1D array with reverse traversal to prevent overwriting needed values
Linear probing in open addressing suffers from clustering, degrading performance; double hashing provides more even key distribution
Exponential search extends binary search to unbounded lists by finding range [2^i, 2^(i+1)) containing target, then running binary search
Trie insertion and search operate in O(M) time where M is key length; no hash collisions and natural prefix decomposition
In bottom-up DP, if current state depends on fixed number of previous states, space can be optimized using sliding window technique
Amortized analysis for dynamic array doubling: total cost of n appends = n (appends) + 2n (doubling copies) = 3n, giving O(1) amortized per append

examples.description: Binary search on answer space. Given bananas and hours limit, find minimum eating speed. Feasibility is monotonic: if speed k works, all k+1 also work.

examples.problem: Capacity to Ship Packages Within D Days (LeetCode 1011)
examples.description: Binary search on answer (ship capacity). If capacity C works, all larger capacities work. Search space is not an array.
Trie insertion inserts each character as node; search is O(word length). Autocomplete via DFS from prefix node.
Classic DP problem. Full table is O(n×W); space-optimized with rolling array is O(W). Reverse traversal prevents value overwriting.
Can compute F(n) in O(1) space by tracking only previous two values; demonstrates space optimization via variable reuse.

analogies.analogy: Binary search on answer space is like finding the highest temperature an engine can tolerate: if 80°C works, 81°C also works. Test the boundary monotonically.
Load factor 0.75 is like a parking garage: too sparse (0.3) wastes space; too dense (0.95) creates chaos. 0.75 balances congestion and utilization.
AVL trees vs red-black trees: AVL is a neat filing cabinet (tightly organized, more lookups faster), red-black is flexible shelving (fewer rearrangements, faster adds/deletes).
Trie is a phone directory organized by first letter branches: you don't search all names, just narrow down by prefix first.
Space-optimized DP using two variables is like a running total: you don't need all past months' budgets, just the previous month to compute this month.

key_numbers.context: Default load factor threshold in HashMap; triggers resize when 75% capacity filled
Maximum rotations required in red-black tree after insertion; AVL trees often require many more
Maximum rotations required in red-black tree after deletion
Coefficient in height bound for AVL trees: height ≈ 1.44 × log(n); red-black is 2 × log(n)
In Java 8+ HashMap, collision chain converts to red-black tree when chain length ≥ 8, improving worst-case from O(n) to O(log n)
Typical load factor limit for open addressing hash tables; separate chaining can go to 100%
Space complexity for Fibonacci DP using two variables (prev, cur); time remains O(n)

common_misconceptions.misconception: Binary search only works on sorted arrays
common_misconceptions.reality: Binary search applies to any monotonic search space where feasibility condition is monotonic. Answer-space search, rotated arrays, and custom predicates all work.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Higher load factor = always better (more memory density)
common_misconceptions.reality: Higher load factor increases collisions and average lookup cost. 0.75 is empirical sweet spot; >0.75 degrades performance substantially.

common_misconceptions.misconception: AVL trees are always faster than red-black trees
common_misconceptions.reality: AVL is faster for lookup-heavy workloads (tighter balance); red-black is faster for insert/delete-heavy workloads (fewer rotations).

common_misconceptions.misconception: Tries are slower than hash maps for string lookup
common_misconceptions.reality: Tries have O(key length) lookup, independent of total keys; hash maps need hash + collision resolution. Tries also naturally support prefix queries.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Space-optimized DP always uses less memory than full DP tables
common_misconceptions.reality: Space optimization requires careful traversal order (e.g., reverse for knapsack) to prevent overwriting values you still need. Order matters.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Doubling array capacity every resize causes O(n) per append
common_misconceptions.reality: Geometric series (1+2+4+8+...) sums to 2n, making total cost O(n) for n appends. Each append is O(1) amortized despite occasional resize.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Open addressing always uses less memory than chaining
common_misconceptions.reality: Open addressing uses contiguous memory (better cache), but must maintain low load factor (~50%) for performance; chaining can sustain 100%.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Amortized O(1) means every operation is O(1)
common_misconceptions.reality: Amortized O(1) means average cost across many operations is O(1); individual operations vary (most are O(1), occasional resizes are O(n)).
